title: Isaiah 51
subtitle: Awake, Awake, Arm of the LORD!

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “The Ransomed of the LORD Will Return”

heb.paragraph: "Look to the rock (ṣūr) from which you were cut and to the quarry from which you were hewn. Look to Abraham your father, and to Sarah, who gave you birth" (vv.1–2). Three "listen to me" imperatives (vv.1, 4, 7). God will comfort Zion’s ruins; the desert will become like Eden (v.3). "My justice will become a light to the nations" (v.4). "The heavens will vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment, but my salvation will last forever" (v.6). "The ransomed of the LORD will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee away" (v.11) — the refrain from 35:10, repeated verbatim.

notes.note: "Look to the rock from which you were cut — Abraham and Sarah." The quarry-metaphor: Israel is hewn from Abraham-rock. One couple became a nation. God can multiply again.

"The heavens will vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment, but my salvation will last forever." Three transient things (heavens, earth, inhabitants) versus one permanent thing: God’s salvation.
"The ransomed will return. Everlasting joy. Sorrow and sighing will flee away." The 35:10 refrain returns. The highway reaches its destination.

refs.note: Paul's Abraham typology — righteousness credited to one who believed — draws on Isaiah's 'look to Abraham' exhortation.
Sarah's barrenness overcome by faith parallels Isaiah's rock-from-which-you-were-hewn imagery.
The ransomed-of-the-LORD-return formula recurs: everlasting joy, sorrow and sighing flee away.
The erasure of sorrow, crying, and pain echoes Isaiah's vision of everlasting gladness crowning the redeemed.

hist.context: Chapter 51 opens with a call to those who pursue righteousness: look to Abraham your father and Sarah who bore you. The barren-rock imagery (v.1) recalls Abraham and Sarah's miraculous fertility — YHWH can make the desert bloom. The comfort motif returns (v.3): YHWH will make Zion's wilderness like Eden, her desert like YHWH's garden. Joy and gladness will be found there. The ransomed-of-the-LORD refrain (v.11) anticipates the return from exile as a second exodus, complete with everlasting joy and the banishment of sorrow.

sections.header: Verses 12–23 — “Awake, Awake, Put On Strength, Arm of the LORD!”

heb.paragraph: "Awake, awake, put on strength, arm (zərōʿa) of the LORD! Awake, as in days gone by, as in generations of old. Was it not you who cut Rahab to pieces, who pierced that monster through?" (v.9). The arm that slew the chaos-dragon and dried the sea is summoned to act again. God as comforter: "I, even I, am he who comforts you. Who are you that you fear mere mortals?" (v.12). Jerusalem has drunk the cup of God’s wrath to its dregs; now God takes it from her hand and puts it in the hands of her tormentors (vv.17, 22–23). "You will never drink it again" (v.22).

notes.note: "Awake, arm of the LORD! Was it not you who cut Rahab to pieces, who dried up the sea?" Rahab = the primordial chaos-monster (cf. 27:1; Job 26:12). The arm that slew the dragon and parted the sea is called to do it again.
"I, even I, am he who comforts you. Who are you that you fear mere mortals?" The doubled I: emphatic. Fear of mortals is incompatible with knowledge of God.

"I have taken out of your hand the cup that made you stagger. I will put it into the hands of your tormentors." The cup transfers: from Jerusalem’s lips to her enemies’. Jesus in Gethsemane asks for the cup to pass (Matt 26:39) — the same cup.

refs.note: YHWH crushed Rahab, the sea monster — the same combat-myth language Isaiah employs for the exodus.
By his power he stilled the sea; by his understanding he shattered Rahab — shared mythic-imagery tradition.
Leviathan, the twisting serpent, will be slain — Isaiah's earlier apocalyptic echo of the Rahab-piercing tradition.
The dragon thrown down draws on the chaoskampf tradition that Isaiah, Job, and the Psalms employ.

hist.context: The 'Awake, awake' summons (vv.9, 17) calls YHWH's arm to action as in the days of old — when it cut Rahab to pieces and pierced the dragon (v.9). This is combat-myth language: YHWH's victory over primordial chaos is the paradigm for his victory over Babylon. The sea-drying imagery (v.10) fuses creation and exodus. The chapter climaxes with Zion's arousal: she has drunk YHWH's cup of wrath to the dregs; now that cup will pass to her tormentors (vv.21–23). The reversal of fortunes is complete.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Lord will surely comfort Zion and will look with compassion on all her ruins; he will make her deserts like Eden, her wastelands like the garden of the Lord. Joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and the sound of singing.ESVFor the LORD comforts Zion; he comforts all her waste places and makes her wilderness like Eden, her desert like the garden of the LORD; joy and gladness will be found in her, thanksgiving and the voice of song.NIVLift up your eyes to the heavens, look at the earth beneath; the heavens will vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment and its inhabitants die like flies. But my salvation will last forever, my righteousness will never fail.ESVLift up your eyes to the heavens, and look at the earth beneath; for the heavens vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment, and they who dwell in it will die in like manner; but my salvation will be forever, and my righteousness will never be dismayed.

rec.text: ""Look to the rock from which you were cut — Abraham and Sarah." The quarry-metaphor: Israel is hewn from Abraham-rock. One couple became a nation. God can multiply again."

rows.label: Awake, Arm of the LORD! — 51:12–23
rows.text: "AWAKE, AWAKE!" The Rahab-slaying arm summoned. The CUP transferred from Jerusalem to her enemies. "Never drink it AGAIN."

hebtext.note: "Look to the rock (ṣūr) from which you were cut and to the quarry from which you were hewn. Look to Abraham your father, and to Sarah, who gave you birth" (vv.1–2). Three "listen to me" imperatives (vv.1, 4, 7). God will comfort Zion’s ruins; the desert will become like Eden (v.3). "My justice will become a light to the nations" (v.4). "The heavens will vanish like smoke, the earth will wear out like a garment, but my salvation will last forever" (v.6). "The ransomed of the LORD will return. They will enter Zion with singing; everlasting joy will crown their heads. Gladness and joy will overtake them, and sorrow and sighing will flee away" (v.11) — the refrain from 35:10, repeated verbatim.

"Awake, awake, put on strength, arm (zərōʿa) of the LORD! Awake, as in days gone by, as in generations of old. Was it not you who cut Rahab to pieces, who pierced that monster through?" (v.9). The arm that slew the chaos-dragon and dried the sea is summoned to act again. God as comforter: "I, even I, am he who comforts you. Who are you that you fear mere mortals?" (v.12). Jerusalem has drunk the cup of God’s wrath to its dregs; now God takes it from her hand and puts it in the hands of her tormentors (vv.17, 22–23). "You will never drink it again" (v.22).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 9: the Rahab-slaying arm, the cup transferred, salvation that outlasts heaven and earth. Mercy at 8: comfort, the cup removed. The 35:10 refrain returns.

coaching.tip: 'Look to the rock from which you were cut' (v. 1) — Abraham and Sarah, one couple from whom a nation emerged. When the present looks impossible, Isaiah says look back: God built a nation from two elderly people in Mesopotamia. If God can do that, exile is not the end. The argument from precedent is Isaiah's characteristic move: past faithfulness guarantees future deliverance.

'Awake, awake, put on strength, arm of the LORD!' (v. 9). The prophet commands God to wake up — an astonishingly bold prayer. But it's not presumptuous; it's liturgical. The psalms of lament use the same language (Psalm 44:23). Isaiah models a faith that is simultaneously reverent and audacious, that dares to call on God's power while utterly depending on it.

prayer_prompt: As the LORD commands 'Awake, awake, put on strength, arm of the LORD,' consider asking God what spiritual drowsiness you need to shake off before His power can move freely through your life.
